title: Chestnut and sausagemeat stuffing
subtitle: The Christmas stuffing, rich and substantial
excerpt: Pork sausagemeat, cooked chestnuts, and sage bound together and baked until golden. Richer and more substantial than sage and onion, and the standard Christmas stuffing in most British households.
sourceNotes: Standard British Christmas stuffing. Chestnut and sausagemeat became the dominant Christmas stuffing combination from the mid-twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before baking. Defrost overnight in the fridge and bake from cold with an extra 15 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Use a larger dish and add 10 minutes to the baking time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 48 hours ahead and refrigerate unbaked. Bake from cold with an extra 15 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking onions gently in butter until soft and translucent without browning. Sweated onions have a mellow, sweet flavour that works in stuffings.
Adding egg or liquid to a stuffing or meatball mixture to hold the ingredients together during cooking and prevent the baked result from crumbling.

content.text: Chestnut stuffing is the richest of the British stuffings, with sausagemeat giving it a dense, satisfying texture and the chestnuts adding a slightly sweet, earthy note. It goes particularly well with turkey and goose. The sausagemeat should be good quality; the stuffing has very little else to hide behind.
Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Melt the butter in a saucepan and
the onions for 12 minutes until completely soft. Remove from the heat and cool for 5 minutes.
In a large bowl, combine the sausagemeat, chestnuts, breadcrumbs, dried and fresh sage, salt, and cooled onions. Add the beaten egg and mix thoroughly. The mixture is the
Press into a buttered baking dish. Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until the top is golden-brown and the sausagemeat is cooked through. A thermometer in the centre should read 75 degrees C or above.

items.symptom: The stuffing is greasy after baking
items.cause: The sausagemeat had a very high fat content that rendered during baking.
items.fix: Use a high-meat-content sausagemeat. The fat should collect at the edges; tilt the dish and drain before serving.

items.symptom: The centre is still pink after 50 minutes
items.cause: The dish was too deep, or the stuffing was refrigerator-cold when it went in.
items.fix: Bring the stuffing to room temperature 30 minutes before baking. Check with a thermometer: 75 degrees C in the centre confirms it is safe.

items.symptom: The chestnuts are too soft and disappear into the stuffing
items.cause: They were very finely chopped or over-softened during cooking.
items.fix: Chop roughly into pieces about 1 cm. Vacuum-packed cooked chestnuts hold their shape better than fresh boiled.

items.symptom: The stuffing has a strong raw sage smell even after baking
items.cause: Too much fresh sage was used without enough dried to balance.
items.fix: The dried sage mellows more than fresh during baking. Use 1 teaspoon dried as the base and fresh as a supplement.

content.text: Add finely grated lemon zest and a small handful of dried cranberries for a brighter, more festive flavour. A tablespoon of Worcestershire sauce stirred in adds depth. For a lighter version with less sausagemeat, reduce to 300 g and increase the chestnuts and breadcrumbs.

Chestnut stuffing became standard British Christmas fare in the twentieth century. The wider availability of prepared sausagemeat made it practical for home cooks. Chestnuts in Britain are associated with autumn and winter, roasted on open fires and stalls from October onwards. Their pairing with sausagemeat in a stuffing is a post-war British invention.
